narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 stood out as the strongest positive factor in the model's assessment of your law school application, as it suggested a solid foundation that nudged the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar exam on your first try—it's close to the 3.24 average among students who succeeded. However, your position in the 2nd decile for Year 3 grades worked against you, pulling the prediction more toward a risk of failure since successful students typically ranked around the 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 25.50 also contributed to that concern, as it fell well below the 37.27 average for those who passed, making the model view it as another hurdle. Overall, these factors led the model to estimate a 43.2% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to help the school maintain high pass rates. While your middle family income bracket aligns with over a third of successful students and your choice to study full-time matches nearly all of them, those didn't carry enough weight to offset the key concerns. Compared to typical admitted students, your Year 1 decile of 3 was lower than their average of 6, adding to the picture of uneven academic performance that worried the model. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's just how these metrics stacked up against patterns from past bar takers. The undergraduate GPA being the top influence shows the model saw real strengths there, but the Year 3 decile and LSAT tipped the balance. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the successful average, while strengthening your upper-year performance through targeted study habits or tutoring. You might also gather stronger letters of recommendation or evidence of academic improvement to supplement future applications. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—your solid GPA gives you a great starting point. With focused effort, you can reshape this story for the next opportunity.
